description.task: Write a Java program that simulates the concept of embedded vs external server deployment using simple Java classes. Create an EmbeddedServer class that starts and hosts an application internally.
instructions: EmbeddedTomcat.start() prints 'Embedded Tomcat started on port 8080', deploy() stores the app name, stop() prints 'Embedded Tomcat stopped'.
ExternalAppServer takes a serverName in constructor; its deploy() prints 'App deployed to external server: [serverName]'.
SpringBootApp.run() calls server.start() and server.deploy() with the app name.
Demonstrate both: first SpringBootApp with EmbeddedTomcat, then with ExternalAppServer.
description.hint: Spring Boot uses embedded servers by default (Tomcat, Jetty), making deployment as simple as running a JAR file.
description.expectedOutput:
Embedded Tomcat started on port 8080
App [MyApp] is running
App deployed to external server: JBoss

description.task: Write a Java program that models the structure differences between a WAR (Web Application Resource) deployment on an external server vs a JAR (Java ARchive) deployment with an embedded server.
instructions: DeploymentPackage constructor takes type, appName, hasEmbeddedServer.
DeploymentManager.deploy() prints different messages for WAR ('Deploying to external application server...') vs JAR ('Running self-contained JAR with embedded server...').
Demonstrate deploying a WAR named 'shopapp' and a JAR named 'microservice'.
description.hint: Spring Boot favors the fat/uber JAR approach — everything needed is bundled in one executable JAR file.
description.expectedOutput:
Package: shopapp.WAR | Embedded Server: no
Deploying to external application server...
Package: microservice.JAR | Embedded Server: yes
Running self-contained JAR with embedded server...

description.task: Simulate a basic server lifecycle (init → start → handle requests → stop) in Java. Implement a simple state machine that tracks server state transitions.
description.context: Understanding server lifecycle is important when building enterprise Java apps — Spring manages lifecycle hooks via @PostConstruct and @PreDestroy annotations.
instructions: init() transitions: STOPPED -> INITIALIZING (print 'Initializing...') -> RUNNING (print '[name] is running').
handleRequest() prints 'Handling GET [path]' if RUNNING, else prints 'Server not running!'.
shutdown() transitions: RUNNING -> STOPPING (print 'Stopping...') -> STOPPED (print '[name] stopped').
In Main, init the server, handle two requests '/home' and '/api/data', then shut down.
description.hint: State machines ensure that operations only happen when the system is in the correct state — this prevents null pointer errors and race conditions.
description.expectedOutput:
Initializing...
Tomcat is running
Handling GET /home
Handling GET /api/data
Stopping...
Tomcat stopped
